revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''仮名暦'''（かなごよみ）とは、主に[[仮名文字]]を使って書かれた[[和暦]]のこと。[[陰陽寮]]で作成されて[[漢字]]を用...'
revision.text:
'''仮名暦'''（かなごよみ）とは、主に[[仮名文字]]を使って書かれた[[和暦]]のこと。[[陰陽寮]]で作成されて[[漢字]]を用いた[[具注暦]]に対して、民間で作成された。'''国字暦'''という表記を用いる場合もある。
当初は単に具注暦を仮名書きにして、その要点のみを記したと考えられているが、後には[[三島暦]]などのように独自の編暦を行う暦も現れた。『[[宇治拾遺物語]]』に仮名暦が登場するが、現存最古のものは[[鎌倉時代]]中期のものである。鎌倉時代末期には[[板本]]の仮名暦も現れた。
仮名暦には主に[[平仮名]]が用いられていたが、[[片仮名]]書きのものや簡易な漢字が混じったものもある。典型的なものは最初に暦序を置き、毎月の最初に1行を設けて、月名や大小を記載する。次に各日の欄があり、上中下の3段に分けられて上段に日付・[[干支]]・十二直を、中段に[[正節]]・[[中気]]などその他の記事を、下段に吉凶・簡単な[[暦注]]などを記した。具注暦は[[]]にのみ配布されその数も限定的であったのに対して、仮名暦は制約がなく、しかも仮名文字か簡易な漢字しか知らない庶民層でも読む事が可能であったために、広く民間に伝わった。日本の暦の普及に大きな影響を与えたのは仮名暦であった。後に京都でも陰陽寮の監修を受けた[[京暦]]が成立した。
ところが、[[貞享暦]][[改暦]]後、全国の仮名暦は[[江戸幕府]]及び[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]の監督下に置かれ、独自の編暦が禁じられた。ただし、[[薩摩藩]]の庇護を受けた[[薩摩暦]]だけがこの禁を逃れられたという。だが、[[1873年]]の[[太陽暦]]改暦によって具注暦ともどもその存立の基礎を失って消滅することになった。
== 参考文献 == *内田正男『暦と時の事典 <small>日本の暦法と時法</small>』（雄山閣出版、1986年）ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 *神田茂「仮名暦」（『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かなこよみ}} [[Category:暦表]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''仮名暦'''（かなごよみ）とは、主に[[仮名文字]]を使って書かれた[[和暦]]のこと。[[陰陽寮]]で作成されて[[漢字]]を用いた[[具注暦]]に対して、民間で作成された。'''国字暦'''という表記を用いる場合もある。
当初は単に具注暦を仮名書きにして、その要点のみを記したと考えられているが、後には[[三島暦]]などのように独自の編暦を行う暦も現れた。『[[宇治拾遺物語]]』に仮名暦が登場するが、現存最古のものは[[鎌倉時代]]中期のものである。鎌倉時代末期には[[板本]]の仮名暦も現れた。
仮名暦には主に[[平仮名]]が用いられていたが、[[片仮名]]書きのものや簡易な漢字が混じったものもある。典型的なものは最初に暦序を置き、毎月の最初に1行を設けて、月名や大小を記載する。次に各日の欄があり、上中下の3段に分けられて上段に日付・[[干支]]・十二直を、中段に[[正節]]・[[中気]]などその他の記事を、下段に吉凶・簡単な[[暦注]]などを記した。具注暦は[[貴族]]にのみ配布されてその数も限定的であったのに対して、仮名暦は制約がなく、しかも仮名文字か簡易な漢字しか知らない庶民層でも読む事が可能であったために、広く民間に伝わった。日本の暦の普及に大きな影響を与えたのは仮名暦であった。後に京都でも陰陽寮の監修を受けた[[京暦]]が成立した。
ところが、[[貞享暦]][[改暦]]後、全国の仮名暦は[[江戸幕府]]及び[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]の監督下に置かれ、独自の編暦が禁じられた。ただし、[[薩摩藩]]の庇護を受けた[[薩摩暦]]だけがこの禁を逃れられたという。だが、[[1873年]]の[[太陽暦]]改暦によって具注暦ともどもその存立の基礎を失って消滅することになった。

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦). Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting..."
revision.text:
{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.
== References == {{Cite-sources-needed|section=1|date=December 2022}} * Masao Uchida, *''Chronology and Time Dictionary: Japan's Calendar and Time Laws''* (雄山閣出版, 1986) ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 * Shigeru Kanda, "Kana Calendar" (*Kokushi Daijiten 3*, 吉川弘文館, 1983) ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6

{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

revision.text:
{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.
== References == {{Cite-sources-needed|section=1|date=December 2022}} * Masao Uchida, *''Chronology and Time Dictionary: Japan's Calendar and Time Laws''* (雄山閣出版, 1986) ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 * Shigeru Kanda, "Kana Calendar" (*Kokushi Daijiten 3*, 吉川弘文館, 1983) ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6

{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].

Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. {{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

revision.text:
{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.
== References == {{Cite-sources-needed|section=1|date=December 2022}} * Masao Uchida, *''Chronology and Time Dictionary: Japan's Calendar and Time Laws''* (雄山閣出版, 1986) ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 * Shigeru Kanda, "Kana Calendar" (*Kokushi Daijiten 3*, 吉川弘文館, 1983) ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6

revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=2022-12}} '''仮名暦'''（かなごよみ）とは、主に[[仮名 (文字)|仮名文字]]を使って書かれた[[和暦]]のこと。[[陰陽寮]]で作成されて[[漢字]]を用いた[[具注暦]]に対して、民間で作成された。'''国字暦'''という表記を用いる場合もある。
当初は単に具注暦を仮名書きにして、その要点のみを記したと考えられているが、後には[[三島暦]]などのように独自の編暦を行う暦も現れた。『[[宇治拾遺物語]]』に仮名暦が登場するが、現存最古のものは[[鎌倉時代]]中期のものである。鎌倉時代末期には[[板本]]の仮名暦（[[頒暦|摺暦]]）も現れた。
仮名暦には主に[[平仮名]]が用いられていたが、[[片仮名]]書きのものや簡易な漢字が混じったものもある。典型的なものは最初に暦序を置き、毎月の最初に1行を設けて、月名や大小を記載する。次に各日の欄があり、上中下の3段に分けられて上段に日付・[[干支]]・[[十二直]]を、中段に[[正節]]・[[中気]]などその他の記事を、下段に吉凶・簡単な[[暦注]]などを記した。具注暦は[[貴族]]にのみ配布されてその数も限定的であったのに対して、仮名暦は制約がなく、しかも仮名文字か簡易な漢字しか知らない庶民層でも読む事が可能であったために、広く民間に伝わった。日本の暦の普及に大きな影響を与えたのは仮名暦であった。後に京都でも陰陽寮の監修を受けた[[京暦]]が成立した。
ところが、[[貞享暦]][[改暦]]後、全国の仮名暦は[[江戸幕府]]及び[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]の監督下に置かれ、独自の編暦が禁じられた。ただし、[[薩摩藩]]の庇護を受けた[[薩摩暦]]だけがこの禁を逃れられたという。だが、[[1873年]]の[[太陽暦]]改暦によって具注暦ともどもその存立の基礎を失って消滅することになった。
== 参考文献 == {{脚注の不足|section=1|date=2022-12}} *内田正男『暦と時の事典 <small>日本の暦法と時法</small>』（[[雄山閣出版]]、1986年）ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 *神田茂「仮名暦」（『国史大辞典 3』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かなこよみ}} [[Category:暦表]] [[Category:和暦]] [[Category:仮名]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

当初は単に具注暦を仮名書きにして、その要点のみを記したと考えられているが、後には[[三島暦]]などのように独自の編暦を行う暦も現れた。『[[宇治拾遺物語]]』に仮名暦が登場するが、現存最古のものは[[鎌倉時代]]中期のものである。鎌倉時代末期には[[板本]]の仮名暦（[[頒暦|摺暦]]）も現れた。
仮名暦には主に[[平仮名]]が用いられていたが、[[片仮名]]書きのものや簡易な漢字が混じったものもある。典型的なものは最初に暦序を置き、毎月の最初に1行を設けて、月名や大小を記載する。次に各日の欄があり、上中下の3段に分けられて上段に日付・[[干支]]・[[十二直]]を、中段に[[正節]]・[[中気]]などその他の記事を、下段に吉凶・簡単な[[暦注]]などを記した。具注暦は[[貴族]]にのみ配布されてその数も限定的であったのに対して、仮名暦は制約がなく、しかも仮名文字か簡易な漢字しか知らない庶民層でも読む事が可能であったために、広く民間に伝わった。日本の暦の普及に大きな影響を与えたのは仮名暦であった。後に京都でも陰陽寮の監修を受けた[[京暦]]が成立した。
ところが、[[貞享暦]][[改暦]]後、全国の仮名暦は[[江戸幕府]]及び[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]の監督下に置かれ、独自の編暦が禁じられた。ただし、[[薩摩藩]]の庇護を受けた[[薩摩暦]]だけがこの禁を逃れられたという。だが、[[1873年]]の[[太陽暦]]改暦によって具注暦ともどもその存立の基礎を失って消滅することになった。
== 参考文献 == {{脚注の不足|section=1|date=2022-12}} *内田正男『暦と時の事典 <small>日本の暦法と時法</small>』（[[雄山閣出版]]、1986年）ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 *神田茂「仮名暦」（『国史大辞典 3』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かなこよみ}} [[Category:暦表]] [[Category:和暦]] [[Category:仮名]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[calendar reform]] of [[1873]], which introduced the [[Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.
== References == {{Cite-sources-needed|section=1|date=December 2022}} * Masao Uchida, *''Chronology and Time Dictionary: Japan's Calendar and Time Laws''* (雄山閣出版, 1986) ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 * Shigeru Kanda, "Kana Calendar" (*Kokushi Daijiten 3*, 吉川弘文館, 1983) ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6

The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], though some versions included [[katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[solar terms|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyō calendar|Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[:en:Calendar reform|Calendar reform]] of [[:en:1873|1873]], which introduced the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

Eventually, with the [[:en:Calendar reform|Calendar reform]] of [[:en:1873|1873]], which introduced the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Kana Calendar''' (仮名暦, Kanagoyomi) refers to a [[Japanese calendar]] written primarily using [[:en:Kana|Kana]] script. It was created in the private sector and used kana instead of the [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]-based [[Guchū-reki]] created by the [[Yin-Yang Bureau|Onmyōryō]]. It is sometimes referred to as the "National Script Calendar" (国字暦).
Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''[[:en:Uji Shūi Monogatari|Uji Shūi Monogatari]]'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], though some versions included [[:en:Katakana|Katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[:en:Solar term|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[:en:Japanese nobility|Japanese nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyō calendar|Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[:en:Calendar reform|Calendar reform]] of [[:en:1873|1873]], which introduced the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.
== References == {{Cite-sources-needed|section=1|date=December 2022}} * Masao Uchida, *''Chronology and Time Dictionary: Japan's Calendar and Time Laws''* (雄山閣出版, 1986) ISBN 978-4-639-00566-7 * Shigeru Kanda, "Kana Calendar" (*Kokushi Daijiten 3*, 吉川弘文館, 1983) ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], though some versions included [[:en:Katakana|Katakana]] or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|kanshi (干支)]], and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as [[:en:Solar term|seasons]] and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[:en:Japanese nobility|Japanese nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyō calendar|Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[:en:Calendar reform|Calendar reform]] of [[:en:1873|1873]], which introduced the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

Eventually, with the [[:en:Calendar reform|Calendar reform]] of [[:en:1873|1873]], which introduced the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyō calendar|Jōkyō calendar]] (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the [[:en:Calendar reform|Calendar reform]] of [[:en:1873|1873]], which introduced the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]], the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=kanshi (干支)}}, and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as {{ill|Solar term|en|Solar term|WD=Q1190993|lt=seasons}} and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[:en:Japanese nobility|Japanese nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|en|Jōkyō calendar|WD=Q2353004|lt=Jōkyō calendar}} (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|WD=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain}}, was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the {{ill|Calendar reform|en|Calendar reform|WD=Q1329260|lt=Calendar reform}} of {{ill|1873|en|1873|WD=Q7757|lt=1873}}, which introduced the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}}, the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.

Initially, it is believed to have been a simplified version of the Guchū-reki written in kana, highlighting only the key points. Later, independent calendars like the [[Mishima Calendar]] (三島暦) were developed. The Kana Calendar appears in ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|en|Uji Shūi Monogatari|qid=Q2446713|lt=Uji Shūi Monogatari|1=Uji Shūi Monogatari}}'', but the oldest surviving example dates back to the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. By the late Kamakura period, printed versions of the Kana Calendar (''Suri-goyomi'', 摺暦) began to appear.
The Kana Calendar predominantly used {{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|qid=Q48332|lt=Hiragana|1=Hiragana}}, though some versions included {{ill|Katakana|en|Katakana|qid=Q82946|lt=Katakana|1=Katakana}} or simple kanji. A typical Kana Calendar started with a calendar preface, followed by a section for each month. Each month's section began with a single line indicating the month name and whether it was a "long" or "short" month. Each day was divided into three tiers: - The upper tier contained the date, {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|qid=Q246658|lt=kanshi (干支)|1=Sexagenary cycle}}, and [[Twelve Directives|junishiki (十二直)]]. - The middle tier recorded information such as {{ill|Solar term|en|Solar term|qid=Q1190993|lt=seasons|1=Solar term}} and [[climatic phenomena]]. - The lower tier contained basic fortune-telling notes and simple [[calendar annotations]].
Unlike the Guchū-reki, which was distributed exclusively to the [[:en:Japanese nobility|Japanese nobility]] in limited numbers, the Kana Calendar had no such restrictions. Its use of kana or simple kanji made it accessible even to the common people with limited literacy, facilitating its widespread adoption. It played a significant role in the popularization of calendars in Japan. Over time, a Kyoto-based version supervised by the Onmyōryō, known as the ''Kyōreki'' (京暦), also emerged.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|en|Jōkyō calendar|qid=Q2353004|lt=Jōkyō calendar|1=Jōkyō calendar}} (貞享暦) reforms, all Kana Calendars were placed under the supervision of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the [[Tsunemikado family]] (土御門家), prohibiting independent calendar compilations. Only the [[Satsuma Calendar]] (薩摩暦), under the protection of the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}, was exempt from this prohibition. Eventually, with the {{ill|Calendar reform|en|Calendar reform|qid=Q1329260|lt=Calendar reform|1=Calendar reform}} of {{ill|1873|en|1873|qid=Q7757|lt=1873|1=1873}}, which introduced the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}, the Kana Calendar, along with the Guchū-reki, lost its foundational purpose and disappeared.
